questions.question: Match each emotion-classification theory to its core claim:
categories: Discrete basic vs complex emotion categories
Each emotion is a point in arousal × valence space
Emotions are dynamic states arising from cognitive appraisal

correct_mapping.Categorical theories: Discrete basic vs complex emotion categories
correct_mapping.Dimensional theories: Each emotion is a point in arousal × valence space
correct_mapping.Component-process theories: Emotions are dynamic states arising from cognitive appraisal

questions.explanation: Categorical theories split emotions into discrete (basic vs complex) categories. Dimensional theories place emotions on continuous arousal × valence axes. Component-process theories treat emotions as fluid states arising from appraisals (goal relevance, urgency, control, etc.) and are rarely tested with neuroscience methods.

questions.question: Match each dimensional model to its geometry:
categories: Boomerang shape — two arms (positive/negative valence) meeting at a neutral endpoint
Circle — orthogonal arousal × valence axes with similar emotions clustering

correct_mapping.Vector model: Boomerang shape — two arms (positive/negative valence) meeting at a neutral endpoint
correct_mapping.Circumplex model (Russell, 1980): Circle — orthogonal arousal × valence axes with similar emotions clustering

questions.explanation: The vector model uses a boomerang shape where arousal is distance from a neutral endpoint along a positive or negative valence arm. Russell's circumplex arranges emotions around a circle with orthogonal arousal and valence axes — relaxed and excited share positive valence but differ in arousal.

questions.question: Match each fear pathway to its description:
categories: Thalamus → amygdala in milliseconds; pre-conscious; triggers fight/flight/freeze/fawn
Thalamus → neocortex → amygdala; slower; detailed analysis with conscious awareness

correct_mapping.Low road (rapid subcortical): Thalamus → amygdala in milliseconds; pre-conscious; triggers fight/flight/freeze/fawn
correct_mapping.High road (slow cortical): Thalamus → neocortex → amygdala; slower; detailed analysis with conscious awareness

questions.explanation: LeDoux's two-pathway model: the low road is fast and crude (priming the amygdala for immediate defense), while the high road is slower and detailed (refining or suppressing the initial response). Intercalated cells gate output between them.

questions.question: Match each patient/lesion to its fear-conditioning profile:
categories: Absent SCR; intact declarative knowledge of CS-US link
Intact SCR; absent declarative knowledge of CS-US link
Cued conditioning fine; contextual conditioning impaired
Fails both cued and contextual freezing

correct_mapping.Patient S.M. (bilateral amygdala): Absent SCR; intact declarative knowledge of CS-US link
correct_mapping.Patient W.C. (bilateral hippocampus): Intact SCR; absent declarative knowledge of CS-US link
correct_mapping.Rat with dorsal hippocampal lesion: Cued conditioning fine; contextual conditioning impaired
correct_mapping.Rat with amygdala lesion: Fails both cued and contextual freezing

questions.explanation: Classic double dissociation: the amygdala underwrites the fear response (S.M., amygdala-lesion rats), while the hippocampus binds the fear to spatial/episodic context (W.C., dorsal-hippocampus rats). Hippocampus is therefore not 'purely emotional' as the limbic system theory would predict.

questions.question: Match each somatic-marker route to its mechanism:
categories: vmPFC triggers actual visceral response via the amygdala
vmPFC simulates somatosensory pattern in insula and somatosensory cortex without a bodily response

correct_mapping.Direct ('body loop'): vmPFC triggers actual visceral response via the amygdala
correct_mapping.Indirect ('as-if loop'): vmPFC simulates somatosensory pattern in insula and somatosensory cortex without a bodily response

questions.explanation: Somatic markers can be triggered directly through the body (body loop, via amygdala-driven viscera) or indirectly by simulating the body state internally (as-if loop in insula and somatosensory cortex). Both flag options before logical evaluation.

questions.question: Match each Iowa Gambling Task deck/group to its profile:
categories: High reward (+$100), occasional very large losses — disadvantageous
Smaller reward (+$50), smaller losses — advantageous
Develop anticipatory SCR; shift to C/D
Lack anticipatory SCR; persist with A/B

correct_mapping.Decks A/B: High reward (+$100), occasional very large losses — disadvantageous
correct_mapping.Healthy controls: Develop anticipatory SCR; shift to C/D
correct_mapping.vmPFC patients: Lack anticipatory SCR; persist with A/B

questions.explanation: The IGT shows that healthy bodies flag risk via anticipatory SCR before the consequence occurs, steering choice toward C/D. vmPFC patients lack this somatic-marker signal and persistently choose the disadvantageous A/B decks even after 80 trials.

questions.question: Match each hormone to where it acts:
categories: Crosses the blood-brain barrier; acts on central glucocorticoid/mineralocorticoid receptors
Peripheral β-adrenergic receptors on visceral sensory endings
Same peripheral β-adrenergic targets on visceral sensory endings

correct_mapping.Cortisol: Crosses the blood-brain barrier; acts on central glucocorticoid/mineralocorticoid receptors
correct_mapping.Epinephrine: Peripheral β-adrenergic receptors on visceral sensory endings
correct_mapping.Norepinephrine: Same peripheral β-adrenergic targets on visceral sensory endings

questions.explanation: Cortisol crosses the BBB and binds central receptors. Epinephrine and norepinephrine act peripherally on β-adrenergic receptors of visceral sensory endings — the very receptors that propranolol blocks, eliminating the emotional memory boost.

questions.question: Match each region in the reappraisal top-down model to its role:
categories: Selects and applies the cognitive strategy
Translates strategy into regulatory control signals
Emotion-processing targets that get dampened

correct_mapping.Dorsal frontoparietal attentional networ: Selects and applies the cognitive strategy
correct_mapping.Dorsal ACC, vlPFC, dlPFC: Translates strategy into regulatory control signals

questions.explanation: Ochsner's top-down model: the dorsal frontoparietal (task-positive) network selects strategy, dorsal ACC + vlPFC + dlPFC translate it into control signals, and these signals dampen amygdala and insula activity. The frontoparietal network is general control — it activates for both 'increase' and 'decrease' conditions.

questions.question: Match each social-cognition theory to its core stance:
categories: Run the experience yourself — mirror neurons as candidate mechanism
Recall accumulated patterns of how people 'like this' usually feel
Treat others as rational agents with goals/beliefs to predict their behaviour

correct_mapping.Simulation theory: Run the experience yourself — mirror neurons as candidate mechanism
correct_mapping.Theory-theory: Recall accumulated patterns of how people 'like this' usually feel
correct_mapping.Dennett's intentional stance: Treat others as rational agents with goals/beliefs to predict their behaviour

questions.explanation: Three accounts of mind-reading: simulation runs others' states in oneself (mirror neurons fit here), theory-theory builds knowledge from social experience, and Dennett's intentional stance is the meta-strategy of treating agents as rational to predict behaviour.

questions.question: Match each Decety empathy component to its brain regions:
categories: Insula, ACC, somatosensory cortex
Parietal lobe, PFC, insula
Medial + dorsolateral PFC
ACC, lateral PFC, vmPFC
questions.explanation: Decety's four-component model: emotion sharing (perception-action coupling in insula/ACC/somatosensory cortex), self-awareness (parietal/PFC/insula self-other distinction), mental flexibility (medial + dorsolateral PFC perspective-taking), and emotion regulation (ACC/lateral PFC/vmPFC). Without regulation, mirroring leads to personal distress.
